speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Mi Lew and Marie Lee Lew took place at their home on the edge of Moscow on December 10th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Do you give credit to anybody that goes broke?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He himself goes broke. You can very seldom get anything out of anybody. I read in the paper where a deputy grant, you know, that owe millions of dollars or something in the Middle West or someplace, their credit was supposed to collect through the store. The store closed up. I think you're going to have a hard time. My father -in -law had a restaurant in Ellensburg. I think all those people there, those hay guys, every spring they cut hay and they work maybe one month, six weeks, and then they pay. But in the meantime, he closed up. I sent a lawyer to ask for a collection. I think $400, I think we got $18 altogether. I said, no use. I had to run all the way from here to go over to Ellensburg to see what he called it. At that time my father was back in China. No matter what you do, you cannot get it because it's awfully hard. I wonder if he expected when he went back.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He was hoping. There's always hope. When a fellow owes you money, he himself didn't go broke. It just happened that the poor time, the timing was wrong. It happened to be that before they paid the yearly expenses, the town closed up on them and they had to leave this particular town in Idaho. So he himself had to leave too, of course.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Another thing too, when the older people go back to China, what little money they have. They probably got it in their little land, bought a little bit of something. It's hard to, if they owe $400 or $500, it's hard for them to raise $400 or $500 in American money and give it back to you. It's awfully hard to do that.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because he had so long gone, although he admitted that he owed it to you, but what can he do?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He hadn't got anything to give it to you. He certainly can't get blood out of a turnip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a very long time that he waited before he wound up going back home to try to collect?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He didn't go back right away. I think he tried to make a go of it in Wenatchee. I think he went back after Wenatchee because it was in the 20s. I think it must be 15 or 20 years later. Because if you go back too early, because most of the people are here still working here, then it's all over the United States. But most of them, or many went back and retired. Some of them retired, some of them passed away. He probably got a few of the close relatives that are close to him, that he knows where they are, and they heard him, but he's doing so well. Then he tried to go back and collect, see if he can. In fact, that's just a part of the trip. Most Chinese, especially from the old country, they're like a salmon. They want to go back to their breeding grass before they die. Of course, he hadn't seen his family for a long time. He hadn't seen the family, hadn't seen the village, hadn't seen anything. He was in the 75 or 80 year old, 70 or 80 year old, before he went back there. So, therefore, he liked to go see the old folks and see how the old village is, then he'd come back and die in peace.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: By custom, I wonder if there's any sort of statute of limitations, like seven years here. No, no, there's no statute of anything. But the trouble is, everybody's hard up. They're poor people. I imagine if these people had made good in later life, they'd be glad to give it to him, whatever they owe him, because it's a debt to the Chinese people. I think, in fact, I can remember that. I think that's the time when China itself was in the war. Between the North and South Chiang Kai -shek's armies, the communism were going there. They were having a real rough time at that time. The war was just going back and forth all the time. Everybody was scared and everybody was hanging up.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: What do they have?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think that's one reason. If it was peaceful, if that fella made it, he probably would have got it. That's the way the Chinese people feel. They will do it, even sacrificing to some point. It's not that they don't want to pay the debt. But if the children are hungry, they're going to use that money first to feed the children first, before they give back to the debt. Or at least a part of the debt, use a part of the money to feed the children. Maybe not the best of things, but at least prevent them from starving to death.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They will use that money, of course. One thing Clara Grove told me when I talked to her. She said that she feels that in the old days, a person's word stood for a great deal more than it does now. That's right. I agree with you. And if they signed something, their signature was worth yet more. That's right. In the old days, the Chinese people were that way too. You don't have to sign it. You just put it in gold. You don't have to sign it. As far as I'm concerned, I had a friend when I was starting out, out to 29, I went down. I didn't have any money either. Then I went down to talk to an old friend of mine, an old gardener. I was doing the old year. I wanted to borrow a little bit of money to keep the rest going.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That was in 31?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, 31. And he gave me, I would say, the last dollar. After he gave it to me, he only had $6 or $7 left. Although he didn't need it because his garden was producing.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: What little did he need from day to day?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Whatever he used from day to day. Then of course, he loaned me whatever, $700. I think I loaned it to him. He loaned me. Then of course, it must be five, six years later, then I paid him back. I paid him back. We don't have to worry about the interest part. The interest part, they don't count. If you want to give him something, okay. If you don't want to give him something, that's fine. The Chinese people are not that, not to say how much interest, but don't know what he called it. That part doesn't count at all. So finally, I paid him back. He was getting old. He wanted to retire. He wanted to go back to China. I said okay, then I paid him back all his money. Then of course, after he went back to China for about six, seven years, then in the village, he had a great big typhoon. These big storms in Florida. Except in China, they call it typhoon. Typhoon blew his house down. It blew part of his house down. Then he wrote and asked me if I could loan him a little money to build back his house. I did. At that time, I was getting on my feet a little bit. I scraped it up. I wasn't rich at that time, but nevertheless, I had a little money. I sent him back some money to build his house back in typhoon. Of course, I knew very well at the time I could not ask him to pay me back because he hadn't gotten any money. He was getting old. He was getting old and he didn't have any money to pay me back. He spent the money already, what he brought back. Therefore, he said, although he told one of his relatives or something, he said if I went back to the village and asked him for it, he would be glad to sign over his house. He didn't give it to me for that debt. He said he'd be glad to do that. Of course, I never expect that of him because I feel that I owe him some too. When I started, I didn't have anything. I feel the same way toward him because when he's down, I like to help him out too. That's how Chinese people, we can borrow money. As a general, we were friends. You can borrow money. It's quite simple because we can borrow from one another. We're something like the Jewish people. If a person has money, you don't have to sign. You don't have to go and have no republic sign all this other stuff to get your money. You can go ahead and ask your friend and the friend who loaned it to you. He'll loan it to you and he'll honor you. He'll pay you back. But of course, you have to know who the friend is. Yeah, you have to know. He can't be a gambler or something like that. You have to know. You've got to use your judgment. You've got to use your judgment. But a good friend, you don't have to sign anything. You don't have to do anything. They'll pay you back. They'll pay you back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could that debt, say, have passed on to his son?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, he can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't want it to or ask for it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, because I knew his son wouldn't have it either because he was in a hard time.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I feel that the good Lord has been kind to me, so why should I press something that way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I feel that way. We don't need what he got. He did us a good deal. What we were in need. When he was in need, we knew that he wouldn't have any chance of repaying us back because he was old then, you see, and he had used up whatever he had saved. He could not go back to work because he was too old to work. I suppose he wouldn't have asked you either unless he really needed it. Yeah, that's right. And he has to need it for something that's important before he'd ask. Yeah, and then I had another friend that was in need, but I knew better, see, because I didn't send it to him because I knew better. One of the friends was a very old friend of mine, too. He was a very good friend of mine. The commonest got a hold of him. A little bit later, the commonest got a hold of him and tried to get all the friends to squeeze money out of the foreign to give it to him. I knew that. I knew that. Although he asked me, he wrote and asked me, but I did not send him any because I feel that it would not do him any good. In other words, it just excused our friendship to give it to him. Because I knew that later years I heard about that. I heard it very much so. That was true. So he was being pressured by the commonest. Yeah, he was pressured by the commonest. And no matter who did try to get the money out of the friends abroad for the commonest people. If he was an old friend, he might have felt himself in a pretty hard position to have to ask you. Yeah, but he asked, he probably, you know, the commonest pressure him to do that, they probably censored him before it came out to me. So he could not express his thoughts to me about that. But I knew about it. I knew he was in pressure because the commonest took over at that time. I knew that. In fact, they pressured my mother too. I didn't send her any. They pressured my mother and beat her up and all that other stuff. I still didn't send her any. She was in her 70s and 80s.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Even then, she sold a piece of property about 10 or 15 years before. The common, common. They say they want that money. She said, well, she's been using that to live on.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Why could she?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They don't care. You better get it from someplace.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: She didn't have any money to give it to them. Well, then they beat her up. She lost a few front teeth and everything. That's terrible. Even in just a small case, many, many of our friends, their family even got killed.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Their fathers and mothers and husbands and wives, they get killed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what? Because they didn't give up what they did?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were on the wrong side?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, that's about it. This is at the beginning of the time where one is going to control the other. If you had an enemy, you go ahead and complain to the civil court of the communist party because the other party is in a head down. You make stories about it like my mother. My mother had a piece of property. In fact, that farmer used to farm our land. She said, we oppressed them. We had to take the food out. They gave us too much rent, too much all that. So, she made a complaint because she didn't like my mother. So, therefore, she made a complaint and then, therefore, the court goes at that time, you see, everybody's on the other side. So, that's what happened. But right now at the present time, it's not that way anymore. They check things out now. Of course, like I said, the older generation passed on now. So, the newer generation, there isn't any trouble anymore. And it's been 25 years too. This Congress started in 1949. 1950, 1960, 1970, that's 25 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the old people had a harder time adjusting to the change?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Of course, the older people had a harder time. Yeah, because the older people, especially now, the older people, especially the influential older people, most of the older people, what they get after are the wealthy older people. See, the wealthy older people, they hate to give it up. I say it right along. Communism, in other words, is getting at the top and moving back down to the bottom and lift the bottom up a little bit. That's why China, without communism, she could not be where she is today because there are too many poor people. There's not nobody in between. They're either too rich or too poor. The in -betweens are very, very few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you say before that's what you saw when you went over there thinking about staying?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, when I was over there thinking about staying at that time, I knew war was coming. I could feel in the air that Japan was in for 930. Japan was about ready to attack Manchuria. 1932. Yeah, because the Chinese government, they go every so often, they pass hand rails out and say, in case an airplane flies over, you got to do this. And if the airplane flies over, you do that. And they tell you how to get out of the bomb shelter and stuff that way. Although they haven't got a bomb shelter, but they tell you to get in the building. The people thought this was coming. Yeah, I could feel it in the air, but the poor people can't. In the first place, the poor people can't read. And they got nothing to lose. That's all they got. It's just that every day, go out and work and maybe get enough to eat, maybe not. So they have to work no matter what happens. I felt it in the air, especially when I come back from the United States and I went back to China. I could feel that in the air, that the war was coming and Japan is getting ready to move into Manchuria. I can feel it. And of course, like I say, I only had one year to stay. If I stayed another six months or so, I couldn't come back. So I had to do one way and make up my mind right away quick. So I decided to come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were there in 1929, was it the way it was?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It was people who were rich and then people who were little. Most people were old people. Up until 1949, it was that way. Until the families come in. It's terrible. That's why I can see where the common is right at present time. It has a great change from what it was. In 1930, I was out and made the same trip in 1930 from Canton to Hong Kong. I made it in 1972, the same trip from Canton to Hong Kong. There is lots of differences. At that time, it was really everything was, you know, people were so poor. And so, you know, everybody's begging on the streets in Canton. It's really filthy and it's awfully bad. But in 1972, I see everything. It's not decorated up in a way you call it like the Western era. But nevertheless, her people, they got a shelter and they got a meal coming. Maybe they hadn't got any steak or anything. But nevertheless, they got something to fill their stomach. That's what you notice now. The people seem to be, like I say, they are quite content, you know. But they would like to have more, but when everybody's the same, they don't fight that way. They don't see any difference anymore. So they're content. They feel that they're doing it for the country. And everybody's the same. They feel good about that way. Where before, like I say, the beggars on the street, you die on there, the rich come along, they kick you. Instead of trying to help you out, they kick you.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because you're worse than the animals. So it's really, it has improved a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who had the power in China in the 20s when you were there? I mean, who had the control?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Chiang Kai -shek was part of it. At that time, they had war amongst the, what do you call it, Chiang Hao -ling and General Fang, you know, all those guys. They were fighting over the territory all over China at that time. The British support one army and the Germans support another army. You know, they fight them themselves like they do in Israel. You know, one side the Russians on one side, the other ones on the other side. Chinese warlords. Yeah, warlords, they call it. They call it warlords. In other words, sometimes some foreign countries support you and then you go ahead and conquer somebody else. That's the way they are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how much of the people were rich? Would it just be a tiny fraction?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In fact, less than I would say maybe 3 or 4 percent, that's about all, that are rich. But the rich are real wealthy though. The rich are real wealthy. They're so rich that they control everything. Even Hong Kong now, the same way. Hong Kong now, you know, the wealthy Chinese are so rich there, they're really, you don't know how much money they've got. You can't tell. Because their taxes are low too. Yeah, their taxes are low too. They only tax... $12 ,500. No matter how much you make. No matter how much you make, you make it. See, that's 12 and a half percent. That's the highest. Yeah, 12 and a half percent. So you go to Hong Kong, if you want to get ahead, you go to Hong Kong and build a business. The most thing, you can make a million dollars, you're going to pay $12 ,500.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the wealth inherited? Is that how it was passed on?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, it was inherited. Passed on from one generation to another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it certain families that were wealthy?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, of course, it's the families that were wealthy. You see, the old Chinese tradition, you know, to be, you know, if you have education, you know, the Chinese people used to have, years ago, you know, they have, instead of an exam, you know, the Chinese in the old time, the people that write the best poetry are the head of the government. It didn't, the person that makes that, how something that produced the most wheat or produced the most rice or something, the way you call it, to give to the people, no. But the people that write the best poetry, that they have every year, maybe every five or ten years, I forgot what it is now, the government, the king, the queen, the dowager now, the king, every so many years, they write a poetry. If you write the most beautiful poetry, they give you a governance of the one section of the thing that you do because you are an educated man, you are a way caller, so therefore you can rule that territory. Then the court, the one that writes the best poetry, therefore he wants his uncle, his nephew, his way of going down the line to do the connection with him because even the Chinese book said, if one of your relatives received the king's signature, a way called poetry, the whole family would get the benefits. That's the old Chinese tradition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it still a part still going on in the mace or was it gone by?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, 20 is still going on. The warlord, you support the warlord, the warlord got all his relatives down here, down the line to run the whole thing, just so long as you get money from him. The city mayor, councilmen, all that. That's where... Of course they make rules to benefit themselves at the people's expense.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what we call patronage, right?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You give out the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they do here, a certain amount?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It's pretty hard to do that to blatant your own relatives. Usually you at least keep your relatives out and maybe put in your friends. Well, the Chinese people are relatives, so they're not friends. Yeah, the Chinese people are more related. They're a family group. They're the way called.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So if you're a real true friend, you really got a friend. That would... Well, you know, that they consider you almost a brother, a relative, if you're a true friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were there in the 20s, were you looking it over to see if you could be an engineer there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I graduated. I went back there to just look over to see if I could get a job, see how things are. Of course, I came over six, you see, and I was 18 years old. I was about 23, about a one -year -old. Yeah, 23. In between there. It was back in 29.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm curious about when you were there then, where did you go to look for a job? Who did you ask?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In China, when you look for a job, you have to know somebody. You have to know people, connections. They're not there. You can get any job. If you do, you'll probably land up there hauling dirt, you know, a bamboo pole with two baskets on it. Yeah, with an engineer, yeah. With a degree. With a degree, you do that. Well, you know, it's not altogether different in this country now. It doesn't look like it, but you do have to... You still have to talk to the guy's shoulder and the other guy's back. That's why I, you know, why Congress now is blaming the Lockheed people, you know, for giving some of the money to the people that, you know, so they buy their plaque, you know, so sign the contract, give them the weight cost. They do that in Washington, D .C. all the time. You go ahead and your congressman go ahead and buy your copy, patch it in the back, except there's such a smaller volume, that's all it is, where you're dealing with millions of dollars over there, maybe two, three, maybe half a million dollars or half a billion dollars.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, then you naturally give half a million or something to somebody in order to tell them, you know, I think maybe lucky is all right, you know, what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: So naturally, they're doing this here all the time. That's a part of their business. Well, people do that all the time here. They do it right here in Moscow. Well, that's human instinct. I don't blame them for that because if you was out on the street, you know, I don't know you, I wouldn't even sit out there and talk to you. So therefore, you come visit a little bit when you come in and then, like I say, then pretty soon if I want something off of you or you or something, then you go and treat a cup of coffee and we say good friend and then pretty soon then we know each other. And then we could deal with the same thing over there, except there's a bigger volume. But here we are jumping that we shouldn't do this. We should do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did you go to to try to get a job?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, I had, firstly, in order to get a job, you have to stay there for a little while. Then pretty soon then you have to know some friends, you know, they have some friends, you know, the Chinese are very good and some friends, you know, suppose my wife friend. Now, that knows your relatives over there and then the relatives go to the other ones and they'll say a good word for you and then they'll go ahead and then they'll say, okay, well, we'll try and see. And part of it, maybe it will come back, end up, maybe it will come up, a certain commission will go back to the, offer your wages, go back to the other guy too. Because you don't know that. If you don't know that, then you're fine because you don't feel guilty. But if you feel that you're supposed to make $500, you got $100, go back to the other one, then you feel bad because if you told you only got $400 to start with, then you feel okay. That's all psychological, the same thing, you know. It does work that way. The Chinese people do that, you know, they, what they call, you can barter or make a little commission on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there any particular official that had to approve you to get, for you to get a job?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yeah, you, you, you, you, you, you, you got a, you got a, you got a, you got a, I was going to get onto the bridge across the Pearl River there for a while. I was going to get in there and get a job there. And, and, and, and in order to, in order to do that,

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: no, no, no, no. If you don't know that the headband, you have to get to the lower one, then you can't go in that way. It's a hard day. It's not easy. If you're relative to that one, you don't need to know anything. You get the job, you can get in there, be the headband. I guess it's the same way. The world's always the same thing, you know.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: If you're relative, you can hire an engineer to do your work for a while. While you get the $500, maybe you give the engineer $300, you know, and then you keep the $200. If you have a few engineers under you, you have enough wages, you know, to support yourself.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Without working too much. Yeah, that's right, that's right. Yeah, that's the way, everything in the world is that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a hard decision for you to make to decide to come back here instead of staying there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, it wasn't too hard, no. It wasn't too hard because in the first place, I hate to see people so poverty, so poor, you know. I hate to see that because I always have a kind of a little soft heart for people that are really poor, you see, that are trying to do something for themselves. But yet they can't help themselves. I do have a little softness toward that way. But I do hate people to come and chisel me, you know, all that way. Like I say, I used to run the restaurant there and some people, they used to be coming to the restaurant to ask for food, you know, lots of times. Every day in the 29th, every day, one or two persons come to ask for food. And a lot of them would say they're willing to work for a little bit, see. But a lot of them won't do one work. Just want a handout. They want a handout, see. And I used to be, it gets me so mad sometimes, you know, that...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: ...work, then give them something to eat, see. And there are a lot of them that will do that and a lot of them won't do it. We always treat the ones that ask for work. Yeah, yeah. I like to do some work to get a meal.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But a lot of people say, can you give me a meal? Can you feed me?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, yeah. Well, those who don't fool around with it. Because that's enough for the type that do want a little work. At least he's willing to work, you know. And if there's any work or not to be done after he eats, that's another thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were a lot of these people that were pretty much begging for food, were they coming just passing through?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think so. They come off the train. Yeah, they're not passing through. Not too many nowadays. No, not many. But at that time there were a lot of people. I think people were uprooted during that time. When we started in 21. They lost their farms. Yes. At that time there were a lot of bread lines in Spokane. Yeah, yeah. But there were a lot of that time. But I remember one or two instances that really are very good. You know, we had a little boy here that one time he comes in. He wasn't, I imagine, a 15 or 16 year old kid. He wanted to do work.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I said, would you want to work a little bit?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He said, yes, he wanted to work. My goodness, so I gave him some potatoes. We're old time, we haven't got potatoes like we do. I put them in a box and throw them in the milk. So we used to buy potatoes. I gave him a little bucket and let him peel the bucket of potatoes. Of course, that wouldn't take that. I feel that doesn't take too much effort. If he was hungry he wouldn't have peeled the potatoes because that isn't that much effort to do. If I go and tell him chop wood or dig ditch and then be so hungry he couldn't do it. But this kid was really a very nice kid. After he peeled the potatoes he was willing to do something else. So I fed him. After he peeled the potatoes I fed him. Then he asked me if there was any other work to be done. He did all the work I wanted him to do. Of course, he did ask for paper and I paid him for the work. I knew that was more than his food worth and I paid him for the work. He was happy and so was I. There are very few, but there are a lot of them that you feed them. Just as soon as you feed them they'll peel maybe a quarter of a bucket and then pretty soon you don't look. They go out to the back door and you don't see them at all. You don't see them at all. They leave the potatoes and leave the way it got there and you leave it there and beat it. So you know the world is funny that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this kid stay at all?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, he was going through. Although we did have one kid that was real good though. At the University of Idaho, I had very good luck. I had two or three boys that were really very good boys. They used to come and we used to consider working one hour for a meal. At the old times we used to work one hour for the meals. We considered all that. These boys here, at that time, I know that one boy, his finger is cut off. I forgot what his name is now. Cliff. Cliff, yeah. He came in after work and I said, sure I can eat some work. He was so good. He worked ten days before I knew that he didn't have a place to stay. He worked at the restaurant. He eats the three meals there. Then he works there at night time when we close up. Then he goes down to the railroad track and the boxcar and sleeps in the boxcar until the next day. Then he comes back. I didn't know that. Until one day, I asked him where he sleeps. He's out there in the railroad track. He was such a good boy. We had a room down in the basement. I said, why don't you go ahead and buy some lumber. You build yourself a little room down in the basement. He did. He's a very handy boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He finished school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, he finished that little room. He got a nice little room downstairs for himself. He studied down there and went to school. He finished one year. He finished that one year, but the second year he didn't come back. He was really a nice boy.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We had many, many students. Girls had to go to the university here and finish school that way. We married off a lot of them. It was a lot of fun. We had married them off. They worked for us. They came in and their boyfriend came in. They probably meet somebody to come in to eat. They'll ask us for our advice. We'd become friends. They finished school and then they get married. They stayed with us until after they get married or after they finish school. We married 10 or 15 of them in our time. We've had the Russians since 1931. We had over 40 years. During that time, there's a lot of things going on. There's a lot of nice people. They saw us on the paper. One of our waitress that used to work for us. Her husband just passed away. He's 65 years old now. They went together when she was working with us. He was one of the Frasier boys here in Moscow. His mother is still living here, Agnes Frasier. His father used to be a tin smith here until he passed away. His brother Bill Frasier was a coach in Gonzaga in Spokane. He himself was watching the water power and also for the city. You see, it was in the 20s. He was in his 20s when he got married to our little waitress. You keep track of them that way. Then they come back and see us pretty good. Every time they come back to Moscow, the rest of them come back to eat with us. At least to say hello. One of the girls owned a trailer court down here on College Street there. She met her husband. She was a divorced woman for years and years. Her husband, the man that came here, he tied steel. He's a very expert. Makes it a clear $60 a day. He's still working there. They own this trailer court and also the one at South Washington there. That trailer court. He was eating at our place. She was a waitress there. I think he was 40 years old. That's the first time he had a bearing. They had a few outs. They come in crying on my shoulder. Somebody's telling her that he was running around with other girls. She was running around with her. She got her and all that. I said, don't listen to any third party telling you. Maybe that girl wanted him herself. I said, if you want to be sure, ask him yourself. He'll tell you the truth. Anyway, they've been married for over 20 years now. Having a good life. Up there at Moscow Mountain. It's a lot of fun to get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you talk about young people coming to you for advice. People that do that. I'm curious about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the 30s or back in those days, were the problems that kids were having or the kinds of things they asked you for advice for, do you think they'd be the same as they'd be today? Or do you think they'd be different? What would be the kinds of things that kids would need advice about?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, you know, jealousy of their boyfriend running around with other girls.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Things like that. In those days, we don't have any drugs. We don't have any smoking and all this stuff that's going on nowadays. I don't think I could cope with anything going on now. Just over that period.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not knowing enough of the things to advise them about now. It seems like you must have... Just old -fashioned ideas and ideals that I have. If they ask me, I tell them what I think of it.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It seems like you must have been good people to work for. To have that kind of feeling for the people who worked for you. We do have that. We ourselves, like I say, we don't drink and we don't smoke. We want our help to be the same thing. And we believe in education.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: What's good for them? For themselves?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We don't try to squeeze the last ounce of energy out of them.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: We try to do the best we can for them. And they try to do the best for us too. A lot of grandmothers nowadays. Vivian Larson over here. She has four or five grandchildren. She used to work too. She worked hard. She worked hard. We used to open until two o 'clock in the morning. And six o 'clock in the morning, from one morning until two o 'clock the next morning. That many hours. We had to have two shifts.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, how many hours is that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Six to six is twelve. Almost twenty -four hours. Just four hours leeway there, you see. From two to four. And, well, they didn't have too much, the family. And she used to work until two o 'clock in the morning. And then she would walk home. Of course, in those days, the girls don't have a car, you know. Not like nowadays. She didn't even have a bicycle in them. And she lived way out there on Morton Street.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: That's way out there in, what do you call it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Past Green Street. Yeah, past. At that time, there was nobody out there. And we don't have any, you know. We didn't have any car either. And of course, my work in the morning and I work at night and I don't drive. And I couldn't even drive her anyway, you know. Even if I do, we do have a car. And she would take some... Sometimes, you know, policemen take her home. Yeah, some dog bones. Well, once in a while, if the policemen happen to be around there, you know. Not very often do they do that. And then she would always have two big dogs. And they come halfway downtown to meet her, you know. And that kind of gives her company. But she worked hard and she got to, you know, stay here.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Sometimes, once in a while, we would make a trip to Spokane and say, Well, you stay here until we come back, you know. Well, she stayed there. Even if she had to stay 24 hours, she stayed there until we come back. And then, of course, the time was closed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was working not both shifts, though, did she?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, just after school. But she was going to school at the same time. She goes to school at the same time, but during the summer, you see, she had all day long to work. So the more hours she worked, the more money she gets. They work by the hours. Most of the waitresses work by the hours. They still do. They're born and they're ours. And the time.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Would you, you mentioned to me a little bit how your folks came down in Spokane, how that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me the story and how they wound up getting the place?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, well, see, like I say, my father used to be an herb doctor in Spokane. Well, after he lost his first son that was born. There are three of us girls born already. And when the first boy came, the fourth child came, and after, I think he died when he was nine months old, of meningitis, spinal meningitis, which is bad at that time. There was no cure for it. Why, he just kind of giving up the medicine part of it. He said, well, I don't feel like being here anymore. I want to move. So we were all packed up and getting ready to move to New York, maybe go into something else. But anyway, he wanted to move away from Spokane. But he had heard about this Palouse country and had never been up this way. So he thought, well, on one of the days when he has quite a bit of time, he would take my mother and the youngest girl here. My sister and I are the older ones. We stayed home doing something or other. And anyway, the three of them came around this part of the country, and then they stopped in Moscow. And my little sister wanted some cookies. So they went into Hough's Cafe and had some coffee and buy some cookies for the little girl. And before they left the restaurant, they bought the restaurant from Mr. and Mrs. Hough. Just that fast, you know, because at that time, Mr. Hough probably, I think he had already just finished law school, and he was going into practice, and he wanted to get out of the restaurant business. He and Mrs. Hough were running the restaurant. And so that's how come our family got into Moscow then. So within a very short time, instead of moving to New York or someplace else, we moved to Moscow and had been here ever since 1926.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father an impulsive man to walk into a place and just buy it straight out like that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, he evidently was at that point because actually he had no job in New York. At the time he was going to New York to look for a job or to look into something, he evidently has a certain amount saved up, you know, otherwise he couldn't move the whole family with the... Well, he sold the place. He sold the drug store. That's right. Yeah, he sold the drug store, so he had a little money. He already sold the drug store in Spokane. So he had a little money. That's right. He had a little money. So he doesn't know what, you know, he hadn't made up his mind where to go. He kind of liked it here. He kind of liked it here. This is a purposed country. All the wheat and the fields and the out in the open, you know, and opportunity too for us three girls with the university here and school. Although at that time I was only 16, so I started with high school here at Moscow High School.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it much because his son, his baby died that he wanted to leave the little family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, I think so. He wanted to change.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He wanted to change. He feel that, well, he was an earth doctor. He couldn't do anything for himself. For himself, yeah, for his son. And the son had to die, you know, without him... He couldn't help his son, you know, in any way whatsoever. But that wasn't his fault. There was no way. No, it wasn't his fault. He got the spinal meningitis and died. But he still felt badly about it. Of course he felt badly because that was his first son too, you see. Yes. So... and baby was only nine months old at that time.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You'd said to me that as an earth doctor it was mostly the Americans that he... That he had patients with American people.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. You know, the earth doctor, they use the heartbeat, you know, on the wrist. That's how they... I just saw him, you know, do the practicing. That's how they can tell what the patient need, what kind of earth the patient need by feeling the pulse that's on the wrist. They don't check as far as I know. That's the way they do it. Usually when you open your... see your Tong, they would. Yeah, the Tong. They look at your eyes, you know. Like, now they say a doctor can look at your hand too. Your hand shows the... if you have any disease or anything like that, you know. Of course the eye shows a lot, you know, a lot of disease and stuff. Just same thing like, well, on the problem of I'm diabetic, I know that they can see that, you know, quite a bit in my eyes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you said that the community of Chinese people was small in Spokane at that time, isn't it?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, it was quite small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how big do you think it was?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I sure don't remember. Somewhere around 300 probably. I don't think there's that many. There wasn't too many gardens. No, hardly any gardens. Most of them are just restaurants. Most of them are restaurants, yes. And a few worked. I don't think there were that many. And maybe one or two hand laundry.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, most of the Chinese are in Walla Walla. They go in truck farming. Truck farming down there. Yeah, they're down there. Most of the Spokane people are usually... I would say maybe 100 to 160 at the most. Quite a few families, no. Just a few families after the whole time. There wasn't too many families either. Right now there isn't too many families. Now there's a couple families now that they're in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And everybody was right in one block?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, not everybody lived there, no. There is a Chinese alley. They have one, two, three, I think three stores. The Wongs have one, Ains have one, and the Lees have one, I know. And I don't remember too many others now. But some of the families live upstairs on these stores. But most of the... I would say quite a few of the resident people would have homes someplace else. Just the store people would, you know, they would own the stores below and work in the stores. And they would be selling Chinese food. It's just mostly Chinese groceries to the Chinese communities.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father's store right in the end of the others?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: My father's store was on Wall Street.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It's in the American community. It's on Wall Street, quite close to the Crescent. It's between Riverside and Spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Spokane when you first got there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were about ten at the time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I was in 1920.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live in the area by the store?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. Well, at first we lived right by the... Oh, on Washington. Yes. Later on we lived right above the... We had the same place, one side of the building. It was a second story building on the second floor. It's where we have... My father has his own business. And on one side we have living facilities there. And on the other side is the offices. So we just lived right there. And he would cook or steep the Chinese medicine in our kitchen. They used to use the glass bottles. They'd sell the different bottles. And they'd use the... Oh, his patient would come in about once a week or once every two weeks, depending on whether they lived in Spokane or whether they lived outside of Spokane. And it was harder to get in.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And these are more or less like tonic, rather than vitamins. And it takes longer to go through the... You take it internally as a drink. And of course it takes quite a while before it gets into the bloodstream. It's not like the vitamins today, that the capsule breaks and then they just go right into the bloodstream.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would he be a steady clientele, a person who would come back and see him time after time after time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes. Like I say, they usually keep them well. Of course, they usually have some kind of sickness before they start seeing him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he treat women as well as men?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Mostly men, no. In those days, the women are less inclined to see Chinese doctors than the men. But there are some women that I say very few, just like in today's politics. There are still a few women in politics.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: There's a friend of mine in Portland, where I went to school there. And he was out visiting me, not, well, Thanksgiving time, when we were talking. And I mentioned that I've been talking to you folks. And he said to me that he knows one old man in Portland who he really likes and is a neighbor of his. And this fellow was an herb doctor. And he's now in his 80s. And he and my friend get together, well, he talks to my friend about it. And he said he returned, he retired about five, six years ago, I guess. And he didn't want any more patients at all. But this one guy, he said, insists on coming back. And I guess he says he gives me $500 twice a year. He comes and gives it to me and I say, I just can't turn away. He just insists that I see him because I saw him for all these years. And he thinks I took good care of him, but I don't want to see him. I'm retired.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, we know a girl's father was an herb doctor in Portland. In fact, both he and his wife were... Dr. Kim, Dr. Kim Lim. Hi, Kim Lim. They have a beautiful home. They've got a beautiful home sitting on the hill there. They've got an elevator to go... The elevator to go up to the first and fourth, because they're sitting on the hill. They've got the elevator to go down from the car to go up to the second and third floor. He still lives there. Yeah, he lives there, but his wife passed away. His wife passed away about five years ago. He's the oldest. He's around 80 now, Mike. It sounds like it could be the same. Yeah, yeah, yeah. He had a real good practice.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It really could be the same. And he owns... Because my friend told me he thinks he was a leader. Yeah, he is. He is a leader. The only thing that the little part of it is a bad part of it. I feel it this way now. I'm no authority at all. But he has one daughter. He has one daughter. And other children too. And he has one son, I think. I think he has one son and a daughter. I think that's all he has. But since his mother passed away, his father wanted somebody to take care of him. They got a beautiful home, they got everything. But he married a friend of ours also. Well, in fact, we know her husband. We know the husband, the young husband better than the girl. We just knew the girl through the husband. The husband's family used to be from Spokane. That's how come this boy and I were kids together in Spokane. And I feel that they divorced. I feel that the father wants somebody to take care of him. And therefore they left a couple of daughters to look after him. Because he's situated in Eugene and he's situated in Portland. So, I don't know. It could be that way. The daughter was very close. The daughter and father were very close. They were married for over 22 years because I think they have two girls and a boy. And all of them graduated from the university already before they were divorced.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They hadn't been divorced very long. We saw him about two years ago. That's the first time we knew that they were divorced. And he told us that she went back home to take care of her father since the mother passed away. I imagine, you know, of course there must have been something between the two. Otherwise, she would not have left him for the father. No matter how much she left the father or how much the father is going to give to her. And now that's my attitude. I think that's a reasonable attitude. That something was going wrong. Yeah, I have enough between the two of them. A wife or daughter is not going to go to the father and leave the husband after 22 years or over 20 years anyway.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I'm curious about how you were raised being a girl instead of a boy. The difference in the attitude that the parents had towards... I was more fortunate because my father was a Christian.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: And he believes that girls are almost as good as a boy. But after having three girls, you know, he would like to have a boy too. And then he having a boy, and the boy, he died like... lost the boy, it make him a little bit bitter, you know, probably. But he still feel that us girls should be educated. And so we had always been a Christian family. And we go to Sunday school and church, and we have been going to school all the time. He believes in educating girls just as much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the difference if he hadn't been a Christian?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: What's the old thing?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think the... Well, I would say being a Christian has broadened his mind a little bit more. But of course, my father had never been...

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, he was a Christian too, wasn't he? Was he baptized in the church and all that?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, he believes in Christianity anyway. At least his father believes in Christianity. And he had brought up my and his sister. And they had gone to school. His sister had gone through school too, you know. And so, I don't know. I think it depends on the individual. But I think being a Christian has a... They meet more with more Western ideas. And I think our Western ideas... You know, I'm in the United States and Europe, you know, European people. They believe in educating their daughters too, as well as their sons. But you remember in the old days, you know, I mean, if there is a choice, if money is short, the boy gets to go to school first. You must remember that.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You have enough money for both her and her brother. So, he became a lawyer and she worked. But in the old country, the traditional way of authority in the family and raising kids... I take it the woman was not... No, was a subservient. Well, as far as I can tell, that's true. Well, that's quite true. A lot of people in the United States are the same way, you know. They feel that, you know, women do not have to be educated much as a husband. Because he's the one that's supposed to bring in the living. And therefore, when you educate a girl, after all, a girl belongs to the boys' side. To the other family. And so they feel that way. A lot of people feel that way. Unless you're in education, the real way, you call it, then you feel that way. But the majority of the people, if you're on the verge of sacrificing something, they let a girl educate. They'd rather not sacrifice, let the boy get educated and let the girl, we call it. Unless the boy can't do anything himself, then they educate the girl. But I don't know. I don't know that. It's an equality business. I don't know whether it's so good or not. Sometimes, you know, we can't be, oh, it's cheap.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Somebody's got to be an Indian, isn't it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but maybe it shouldn't just depend on whether you're a woman or a man. You know, which way you're going to be achieved. Well, you know, after all, I believe, you know, Mary did it. It should be a function together. It should be a function together.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But somebody should have something to say about it rather than they say, how come you've got to say I haven't got anything to say?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That isn't right either, you know. You know, because when you have two equalities, you have to be, how come you don't, no, I don't do it. But then you're conflict in one another right there. Somebody, I believe if a leader is good, we should respect the leader instead of trying to tear him down, then I can do the same thing. You know, I believe in that, you know. But you can't be all, everybody, me and me, somebody's got to do the other part, you know. As long as he's fair and honest and try to, you know, do it, you know, I think it really, it's better that way than trying to, everybody trying to be one to be the head man and nobody wanting to do anything else. Then you sit there and fight one another, see.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Don't you think that that's the way it seems that way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I think, you know, the communists right now in China, they feel that everybody should do something to benefit everybody rather than we just do something for ourselves and then we just put ourselves ahead and then let the rest of them, you know, go.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, I don't think that's very good either. That's one thing about the communists today in China, that is, they go for the country first and then themselves next. Well, you know, that is a long way from the way we were brought up, you know. We always say we first, then the family, and then the community, and then the country as you get our father. Well, there are good things in their idea too. Everybody has to do something for the country.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I'd like to say, well, for their way of thinking, I myself can't conceive it quite that far yet. I don't think I could ever turn to say the country first and myself next. I guess I'm just a little bit too individualized. And you've always lived differently. We were brought up differently. But they are changing, though. I mean, they're really feeling that way. When you talk to them, that's the way they feel for the country first, you know. I think that's what, and they do do it, they really have their heart on it too, you know. They just don't just say it. They do it. Country first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, like within a family, though, the way the work was divided up in your family, was it fairly divided?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You know, this is something that young people ask a lot nowadays. Whether the man and wife share responsibilities. For example, I know that with a lot of Swedish families, the women were expected to do an awful lot of that heavy work. It's surprising. And all the milking of the cows. Swedish men didn't like to milk cows. It's a rule. Yeah, yeah. And of course all the housework and all the cooking. Of course, those were all things that the women always did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it that way in your families, too? Were the women expected to do all the cooking and all the domestic kind of work?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, not in our families so much. Well, for one thing, you know, we live in a different community. Although we've been Chinese, but we were becoming very fast Americanized. We live like the other people do. We go to church and see what the church people do. We go to school and see what the other families do.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: As far as my family is concerned, we have been very much go along with what's going on in the other families. We take after the other families pretty much so. And taking after American rather than Chinese families. Yes, I think we do because we live among more American, more time with American side of it than we do with Chinese people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Spokane? Was it that way?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: In Spokane, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did your father become Christian? Do you remember?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, he become Christian after he got over here the first time. I would say I was born 1910, somewhere around the first time he was over here. He was connected with a church in Spokane, a central Christian church in Spokane. I imagine he being a young man, he go to the church and some of the older ladies from the church or family ladies, they take him under the wing more or less. He's the same thing, only he started when he was younger. He started when he was 6, 7 years old in Walla Walla. The church women, the ones that have families, probably take him under the wing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That happened to you?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: When he didn't know English, they were teaching them maybe one hour after school or whatever at the church. I know it was during the time when he was in Spokane the first time. When he first come over, he become connected with the church people. Of course, when we come over, we go right in with the central Christian church in Spokane. When we moved from that church in Spokane, we moved over here to Moscow where we started in with the Christian church on 3rd Street here. That is my family, the Lee family. Then later on when we were going to high school and college, we met most of our old friends were going to the Methodist church, so we kind of switched to the Methodist church. That's how come we're with the Methodist church now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like when you first came to Spokane?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: You were already 10, so you pretty much half grown up in China.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came over here, what was it like to make the adjustment to a brand new country?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It wasn't so bad. The language was the hardest part of the adjustment. Learning English was the hardest part. Of course, my father knows quite a bit of English at that time. Then we have one of the church lady come and teach us in the evening after school to teach us some English too, out of simple English books. In the meantime, I was going to grade school too. I started with the first grade. I was 10, so I was a lot bigger than these sixth graders. I always have to have the biggest lesson in the classroom and all that.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It seemed to be quite a while before I got the hang of the English, but once I got started, it was very easy. Probably, I imagine, about six months at the most. I was quite fluent in understanding English. I can go to a store and buy whatever I want by myself. Before that time, I have to have somebody else go with me or I have to do some pointing.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: It was very easy, though, for children to learn the English. A lot easier than my mother now, who was quite a bit older. She still doesn't speak much English yet. Even after being over here for so long already, it was hard for her to learn because in the first place, she didn't need it. Any time she wants to buy something, she either has one of us go with her to buy it or we buy it for her. So consequently, if a person doesn't need it, they don't need to learn. So it's slower to learn. And of course, when they're older, they're harder to learn, too.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I've heard the same thing exactly from homesteading families from Norway and Sweden. The mother, being at home, didn't have the opportunity to hear a lot of English spoken. Talking to people from your own country, you talk the native language. That's right. You don't need it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about other Chinese kids did you play and spend much time with them?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Not anymore than with American kids because there weren't too many Chinese people.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Like I say, in the whole grade school, I would say there were three families with children. The Aims and the Wongs and us. So there weren't too many more American kids than the Chinese kids that I played with. And not living down in the Chinese alley, you know. I had a lot less chance to play with the Chinese children than I did with American people.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, that must have affected getting you learning the new ways quicker. Yes, it does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They did that, you had that happen to you when you were in Walla Walla, the church?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, the church, that was later. I was over here when I was six, so I was kind of going to get going. The grade school, I started first, I didn't know very much. I had a friend that I played around with and pretty soon I picked it up easy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: American?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: American friend. Then of course the American friend was, before my father brought me over, my father used to know their family pretty well. So I go and he and the family got a son, just about my age. About six years old, we're both about the same age. So we go and I go up there and play and stay. I stayed up there last time for a week before coming home. So I just grew up amongst them. His mother was so good to me, we used to have two little kids, we used to take baths together. Bathtubs, one in one the other. Mother used to wash it, you know, we just grew up together.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: At that time there weren't very many Chinese kids here?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, I was the first one.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You were the first one?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, a year later, I think there's another one, Leland Lee came, that's all. For a long time there was just two of us. Then about four or five years later, then there's about 25 or 30 of them came, all one. I wonder why all of a sudden. Well because of the law, more lenient. At that time the law was very strict. In the meantime, during that time there was a war going on in that time, 1918, around there. Between the war time, nobody came. After the war, then the people started to come again. Because there were no ships there on the Pacific Ocean in the first place. During the First World War, because all the ships were over there in the Atlantic, either shipping ammunition or the soldiers or whatever, I suppose, maybe that's the reason why. Anyway, the law was a little bit more lenient and the people would come because, like I said, there was a war going on at that time. From 1914 to 1918, there was a war going on all the time. Even though the kids came, the mothers didn't bring any of them. No, there weren't a lot of mothers coming at that time. Mothers won't come until quite a bit after that. The mothers won't come. Of course, the Chinese government won't let them come either as far as I can tell. They both come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say that you scrapped with the Italian kids a lot, the two groups?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, we used to fight with them. Not too often, but we do. Some of the Italian kids get cocky, probably, and the Russian kids usually. The Russian kids used to be called the Russian town down there in Walla Walla, that district there. They're a big husky and they're rough, the Russian town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the old Russians come from Russia?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yes, all from Russia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of work do they do there?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They do labor, farm work and things like that in Walla Walla.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you kids run around and pretty much become like a gang?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No, no, no. We all go to school. I don't mean in a bad way. No, no, no. Because you see that Chinatown is a certain district that goes to that one school. You see, all the kids in that district from Fifth Street on up, they got to go to that one school. So all that Chinese group go to that one school because they live in that district.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: No gang at all.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: You mean most of the Chinese?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They live in that building. They live on that one block, what they call Chinatown. Because, and so that's where the kids are. The kids all go to that little school. The bakery school is called. And they don't have any homes outside of that one block. This block has a four story or something like that in it. No, it's a two story. Two story, got about 12 stories in it.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: But they all live in an apartment upstairs. So they all go to school and have one in the section. They usually drag off. But as a general rule, when the kids pick on them, then of course the older, some of us are older. Some of the Chinese boys come on when they're older. So they usually, if they pick on the little kids, then the older ones will help the little ones out. Stuck together. Yeah, a little bit more so than the others would. Well, for a second, we knew it here one time. When Bob, you know, that's the... Oh, of course, that's only...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: When my brother was a little boy, when he was scrapping with another kid, another kid pick on him, you see. And then my nephew, who's a little bit older, John is a little bit older. Naturally, he'd go protect my brother. It was the same thing, but in the same way. And then in this book, it says a lot about the Kliip, you know. A Tong, a kliip. It's not a hong. Kliip is a district. I know it's a district, but they call it, you know, in this book, they've got a few things that are not quite correct. Kliip is a district. It's four counties. Actually, the Cantonese, most of them are... The Idaho Chinese are from Kliip and also older ones that come to San Francisco, Seattle, Portland, and Vancouver, B .C. This whole district is... Tohoku, yeah. Kliip, kliip, four counties. And that is not a Tong. A Tong is a... hipsen and hapsen is a Tong. And they really...

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: they kill one another. They murder one another when something goes wrong. Oh, that's a Tong. That's a social Tong. But they pick up... They're not social Tong. They kill each other too when they have to. Oh, yeah, but they are considered social Tong. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is a Tong?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: I read the book... It's a club. It's a club. They're like the elks. They're like the elks now. The elks accept... ...accept down the elks and then the eagles, the way they call it, accept the older types. They get into trouble. The eagles go and shoot the elks. There's something like that. But there's no family. You don't have to be saying family to be in the same town. It cuts across families. Yeah, no family town. It's a club.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long tradition is there behind the Tongs? Is that an old, very old custom?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They only have the Tong over here in the United States. Yeah, only United States. This society is only in the United States. Because there are many American lawyers that work for these and they're trying to get into the gangster outfit. They want to run certain things. They want something underhand. The American lawyers help them. Since the American lawyers are making so much money off of it, so they don't care if they're so long as the Chinese shoot Chinese, it doesn't make any difference. It doesn't bother them any, so go ahead and shoot Chinese. But the lawyers get them out of it. But since 1900 and I think, let's see, 30 or 28 or something around there, the American passed a law, I think it is. But they cannot murder each other.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Anybody that murdered one another from one Tong to the other, they sent the head man back. As I said, the guy, what they call a hatchet man, they're going to send the head man back. After that, no more Tong. The head man don't want to go back. So after that, no more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the reason for having the Tongs in the first place?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Just a social gathering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How does that escalate into killing?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They go gambling with it. They start gambling. Like an Al Capone, you have a drinking, then they have a gambling, then they have a weight loss. They have a Tong, they're getting all the members together, then they have gambling. Then probably some of these guys here, some of the rich guys go there and gamble. So the other guy is trying to come over here and try to get some of the rich guys gambling over there. So they're invited, that's all. And since the Chinese killed the Chinese, the law doesn't bother them anyway, so they let them do it. And the American lawyers get them out because they didn't have nothing to do. Because they're getting money. You get money out. So until they find that law, they're going to send the hit man back, then after that they quit. No more. The hatchet man is the one that goes out and kills him.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: They usually throw a hatchet. I imagine that's how it comes in. They call that gangster law. Nowadays they call it a hit man. They call it the hatchet man in China.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of the men say in Walla Walla, how many of them would have belonged to the Tongs? Would most of them have belonged to one Tong or another?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Well, I think in Walla Walla, most of them, just one Tong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's one Tong?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Spokane only got one Tong too. Spokane got Habsing, Habsing. I think Walla Walla, Bingkeng Tong, I think that's all. But when they have a war, you see, then they have Bingkeng, Habsing got a war, then the Spokane and the hatchet men go down to Walla Walla and kill somebody down there, and the other one sends them up there. That's what it is. Both of the Tongs are usually the people that are... Ladies don't want to be a gangster kind of type. They don't want to get the other guy to work. Unless you're awfully wealthy, they'll force you in, try to force you into the thing. And if you're poor, they don't want you in there anyway. But unless you make some money to pay them due, then they'll give you protection, they call it. Oh, it's just a racket, that's all. It sounds like something that happened in different form with a lot of groups when they came over here. Yeah, to protect themselves. They do a lot of them, they do that to protect themselves. But now they've got another Chinese, what they call a Tong, where the four families join together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a good one, see? That's back here in China, see?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Those where the four families get together. Four small families. Yeah, four small families get together to protect each other, to help each other out. Those are the ones they don't fight. They don't fight at all. They just more or less help one another out. That's a three...no, no, no.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's not the three. That's a district.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: That's three clans. Yeah, those are different. But these Tongs here, what they call it, more or less kind of like a gang.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those Tongs in China, are they like what you were telling me about when I was here before? Like the families, like the Lu family?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, the one that's in the Kingdom. An alliance. That would be called a Tong too, but it's a different kind. Were these Tongs here in the United States, did they have a lot of, like clubs do, did they have certain rituals and social... They have their conventions. Yeah, they do have those regular families, Tongs. I told you they're family ones. They don't fight. They help one another. And, you know, in case you get in a little trouble with the police or something like that, you know, a little minor trouble, they'll help you out. If somebody picks on you or something, what you call it, they'll help you out that way. And of course, each one member of the family is supposed to go on a visit and then they're supposed to pay him a little dues. It's to kind of maintain the building. Although they do have buildings themselves, they do maintain the building. Everybody in the family is supposed to donate a little bit, you know, to help them maintain the building. Maintain the maintenance of... And the older people, when you get old, you can live there. You like a lot, you know, you like a lot. You pay your dues, then when you get old, you can stay there. They got a little room for you, then they got a little kitchen for you to cook and you can stay there if you want to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This in America too?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, those ones you don't hear. These are the ones that they don't make headlines because they don't shoot one another, they don't kill one another, they don't make headlines. Those are the ones that stay home, they wake up, they help one another. Well, you know, in that book, the Tong disputes that she mentions in there sound like they really were dividing the communities pretty badly. You know, there's a lot of fear going on. When they're fighting, there is a lot of fear. Yeah. There is a lot of fear. They all close up their businesses, you know, they shut up and everybody stays indoors.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Because if you're out on the street, even if you yourself have nothing to do with the thing, if you belong to the Tong, they kill you anyway. I mean, because you belong to the Tong, they just want to get rid of you. I wonder how they even know which Tong you're... Oh, they know the members. Oh, yeah, they know. The members. Of course, you know, the small fry, you know, they don't pay attention to, you see, it's the big fries that you got your name mentioned and this and that, you know. They know you, you know. Small fry can't do anything anyway, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If he had any money, no power, what can he do?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: He can't do too much.

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: When you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what made you decide to come to Pullman? You just graduated from high school at the time?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Yeah, I made it come to Pullman because Pullman was the cheapest one for me to go. But the University of Washington and the Net was the cheapest one for me to go. And the closest home, you see. You know, at that time, money was hard to get and going to school was quite expensive, you know. And, you know, that's how I feel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you been a pretty good student in school?

speaker: Mi or Marie Lee Lew
words: Oh, I'm not that. I was a little bit above C, maybe, somewhere around there between C and B. I'm not an excellent student, but I was a way college, that's all.

speaker: Marie Lee Lew
words: It's about four. It's okay. Well, it's been fun.
